extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds on every reachable execution.

Invariant argument: The only transition that sets sent := true is [t_send_allowed] on send_attempt, which is guarded by "not opted_out". The only transition that sets opted_out := true is [t_optout], and it simultaneously sets sent := false. opted_out is monotone — no transition ever sets it back to false.

Consider any reachable state with opted_out = true. For sent to be true, some transition must have set it true after opted_out became true (since t_optout itself zeroes sent). But the sole sent-setter t_send_allowed requires not opted_out, which is impossible once opted out. Meanwhile the opted-out send path [t_send_blocked] (guard opted_out) explicitly sets sent := false, and every lock transition (adv_* and rst_*) also sets sent := false and never clears opted_out. Therefore in every state with opted_out true, sent is necessarily false.

The combination-lock machinery (stage 0..12, keys advancing stage 0->1 on key_2, 1->2 on key_1, etc., with all non-advancing keys resetting to stage 0) is irrelevant to this property: stage transitions only modify stage and always zero sent; they never set sent true and never reset opted_out. So no stage configuration enables an opted-out send. The two send_attempt transitions have mutually exclusive guards (not opted_out vs opted_out), so an opted-out send_attempt always takes the blocked branch (sent := false). No reachable sequence (within or beyond 15 events) produces opted_out = true with sent = true. Property holds; no counterexample exists.
